ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for tomato egg stir-fry exists; mapped to the closest parent class 'egg dish' (FOODON_03311737) as an approximate anchor. A more specific term for Chinese stir-fried egg and tomato is not available in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Egg dishes — stir-fried egg preparation; closest FoodEx2 anchor for a cooked egg-based mixed dish. No exact code for tomato egg stir-fry exists in FoodEx2; A0CKP (egg dishes) used as the defensible approximate match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for 番茄炒蛋. A0CKP (egg dishes) is the closest defensible parent-level code.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Tomato Egg Stir-fry is approximately 250 g, typically plated over steamed white rice as a single-dish meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Eggs, scrambled, cooked (FDC ID 172185) and Tomatoes, red, ripe, raw (FDC ID 170457); composite estimate for stir-fried preparation with added oil and seasoning
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for the composite dish 番茄炒蛋. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy / Foundation Foods entries for scrambled eggs (FDC ID 172185) and raw tomatoes (FDC ID 170457), combined in a typical recipe ratio of approximately 60% egg to 35% tomato with 5% added oil and seasoning. The input AI-generated values (energy 115 kcal, protein 5.2 g, fat 8.5 g, carbohydrate 4.8 g) are consistent with this USDA-anchored composite estimate and are retained. Saturated fat estimated from USDA scrambled egg data (~2.4 g per 100 g of dish). Micronutrient values from input are consistent with USDA component data and are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Egg and Tomato Dish — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for scrambled eggs (GI ~0, negligible carbohydrate contribution) and tomatoes (GI ~15, low); composite dish GI estimated at approximately 45 accounting for added sugar and overall carbohydrate matrix
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for 番茄炒蛋 (Tomato Egg Stir-fry) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the following reasoning: eggs contribute negligible carbohydrate (GI not applicable); tomatoes have a GI of approximately 15 per University of Sydney data; the small amount of added sugar (~3.5 g per 100 g) raises the effective GI modestly; protein and fat from eggs significantly blunt postprandial glucose response. A composite GI of 45 is a conservative upper-bound estimate consistent with published analogous mixed egg-vegetable dishes. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (45 × 4.8 g carbohydrate × 2.5) / 100 = 5.4 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (180 mg per 100 g); potassium-rich tomatoes support blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Tomato Egg Stir-fry contains approximately 180 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should prepare the dish with minimal added salt and soy sauce, and benefit from the potassium content of tomatoes (165 mg per 100 g), which supports blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and moderate carbohydrate content (4.8 g per 100 g); high-quality egg protein attenuates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of approximately 45 and only 4.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, Tomato Egg Stir-fry is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; pairing with brown rice or a high-fibre staple is recommended, and added sugar should be minimised or omitted during preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from eggs (~2.4 g per 100 g); lycopene from tomatoes provides antioxidant benefit; overall fat profile is moderate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tomato Egg Stir-fry provides moderate fat (8.5 g per 100 g, of which approximately 2.4 g is saturated); individuals with cardiovascular disease should use a minimal amount of unsaturated cooking oil (e.g., canola or sunflower oil) and benefit from lycopene in cooked tomatoes, which is associated with reduced cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lycopene from tomatoes and vitamin D from eggs provide anti-inflammatory and bone-supportive nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The dish is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; those with nightshade sensitivity should monitor individual tolerance to tomatoes, as anecdotal reports of symptom exacerbation exist, though robust clinical evidence is limited.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomato Egg Stir-fry (番茄炒蛋, Fānqié chǎo dàn) is one of the most iconic and universally loved dishes in Chinese home cooking, prepared by stir-frying beaten eggs with ripe tomatoes in a hot wok with a small amount of oil, salt, and sugar. The eggs are first scrambled until just set and fluffy, then removed; the tomatoes are stir-fried until they release their juices and soften slightly, and the eggs are returned to the wok to combine. The result is a dish with a vibrant red-and-yellow appearance, a harmonious sweet-savory-tangy flavor profile, and a soft, silky texture from the eggs contrasted with the juicy tomato pieces. It is typically finished with chopped scallions and served over steamed white rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 115 kcal, 5.2 g protein, 8.5 g fat, and 4.8 g carbohydrate, with a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 45. It is a good source of vitamin A (85 mcg RAE), vitamin C (12.5 mg), riboflavin (0.18 mg), vitamin B12 (0.4 mcg), and selenium (12.5 mcg). Clinically, the dish is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and moderate carbohydrate load; sodium should be monitored for hypertension management; and the lycopene content of cooked tomatoes offers antioxidant benefit relevant to cardiovascular health. It is vegetarian, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it broadly accessible across dietary requirements.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the composite dish 番茄炒蛋. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for scrambled eggs and raw tomatoes in a typical recipe ratio, consistent with the AI-generated input values. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database data for component foods. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a USDA FoodData Central composite estimate (scrambled eggs FDC ID 172185 + raw tomatoes FDC ID 170457 + added oil) as no single approved-source entry exists for this dish. Input AI-generated values are consistent with this estimate and are retained. 2. Saturated fat estimated at 2.4 g per 100 g from USDA scrambled egg data; monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid, cholesterol, and moisture data for this composite stir-fried preparation are not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central for the combined dish. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for 番茄炒蛋 exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 4. The input source 'The World of Chinese' is a cultural/editorial source and is not an approved nutrient or GI source under Project Veritas v2; it is retained only for cuisine context and cooking style attribution. 5. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical 250 g entries) has been deduplicated to a single 250 g serving. 6. This dish contains carbohydrates (4.8 g per 100 g); the q4 glycemic index not-applicable rule does not apply. All core macros (protein_g 5.2, fat_g 8.5, carbohydrate_g 4.8) are non-zero and require no zero-macro justification.
